text:
🇹🇭 THAILAND — OWNERSHIP STRUCTURE
1. Why this matters
Defines legal ownership rights, restrictions for foreign buyers, and permitted property structures under Thai law.
2. Legal Ownership Framework
(Official Fact)
Thailand distinguishes between:
Land ownership
Building / unit ownership (e.g., condominiums)
(Official Fact)
Land ownership is not permitted for foreign individuals, except under limited statutory exceptions.
Ownership rights for foreigners are therefore limited to specific legally permitted structures.
3. Foreign Ownership Rules
(Official Fact)
Foreign nationals are prohibited from owning land directly in Thailand.
(Official Fact)
Permitted ownership structures include:
Condominium units, subject to statutory limits
Leasehold rights over land or property
4. Condominium Ownership (Critical Rule)
(Official Fact)
Under Thai law:
Foreign ownership is limited to 49% of the total saleable area within a condominium building
(Official Fact)
Conditions include:
Funds must be transferred into Thailand in foreign currency
Compliance with condominium registration and regulatory requirements
5. Leasehold Structure
(Official Fact)
Foreign nationals may acquire rights through leasehold arrangements:
Maximum term: 30 years
Renewal: may be contractually agreed but is not guaranteed under law
6. Leasehold Risk
(Advisory Caution)
Leasehold rights expire at the end of the term
Renewal depends on:
Contractual provisions
Legal enforceability at the time of renewal
Leasehold does not confer ownership of land
7. Alternative Structures (Legal Risk Note)
(Official Fact)
Ownership via Thai companies is subject to:
Foreign ownership restrictions under Thai law
Regulatory scrutiny by authorities
(Advisory Caution)
Use of nominee structures to circumvent ownership restrictions is not legally permitted
Such arrangements may be subject to enforcement action and invalidation
8. Advisory Caution / Red Flags
(Advisory Caution)
Land ownership is not available to foreign individuals
Condominium ownership is strictly limited to 49% per building
Leasehold rights are time-limited and carry renewal uncertainty
9. What Needs Legal Confirmation (Live Case)
Foreign ownership quota availability within the specific condominium
Compliance with foreign currency remittance requirements
Leasehold contract structure and renewal provisions
Legal structure used for acquisition
10. Title Registry Authority
(Official Fact)
Property ownership is recorded and administered by:
Department of Lands (Thailand)
(Official Fact)
Registration is required for legal validity and enforceability of ownership rights.
11. Sources
https://www.dol.go.th
https://www.rd.go.th
https://www.boi.go.th
12. Verification Date
09/04/2026
13. Escalation Note
Not Required –
Ownership framework is clearly defined under Thai law. Constraints relate to legally permitted structures, not unresolved legal ambiguity.
